text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Prostate Cancer / Metastatic Hormone-Sensitive Disease

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
DEPARTMENT: Genitourinary Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Return visit for routine disease monitoring. Patient presenting for 12-month follow-up on ADT plus abiraterone for metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC). Established patient.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski is a 69-year-old Polish-American gentleman with a history of Stage IVB prostatic adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, Grade Group 5, cT3bN1M1b) with bone metastases (L3, L4, right iliac crest, right sacral ala) and pelvic lymphadenopathy diagnosed approximately 12 months ago when PSA was 187.4 ng/mL. He was initiated on androgen deprivation therapy (leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months) combined with abiraterone acetate 1000 mg orally daily plus prednisone 5 mg twice daily for mHSPC at the time of diagnosis.

At his 3-month follow-up, PSA had dropped dramatically to 4.2 ng/mL (97.8% decline), testosterone was castrate (<10 ng/dL), and bone pain had improved significantly from 7/10 to 2/10. He was tolerating his regimen without significant side effects.

He returns today for his 12-month assessment. Overall, he reports feeling reasonably well. His primary concern today is persistent fatigue, which he rates as mild and notices particularly in the afternoons. He has been largely sedentary compared to his working years as an ironworker, though he continues to take daily walks around his neighborhood when energy permits. He denies any new bone pain -- his low back discomfort has remained minimal to absent over the past several months. He denies any hematuria, obstructive voiding symptoms, dysuria, or lower extremity edema. He is being followed by his primary care physician for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Blood pressure has been well managed at home per his report.

His last leuprolide injection was administered approximately 6 weeks ago and is due for renewal today. He has been taking abiraterone and prednisone consistently with meals each morning and has not missed doses to his knowledge.

A referral was discussed at today's visit for germline genetic counseling given his family history (father died of prostate cancer at 78; paternal aunt with ovarian cancer) and the biological plausibility of an inherited cancer predisposition syndrome. Patient expressed openness to this.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Mild fatigue (Grade 1, no impact on ADLs), occasional mild hot flashes (pre-existing, consistent with ADT, managed with behavioral modifications).

Negative: No bone pain, no new musculoskeletal complaints, no hematuria, no dysuria, no urinary obstruction, no lower extremity swelling, no chest pain, no dyspnea, no abdominal pain or discomfort, no nausea or vomiting, no diarrhea, no constipation, no peripheral edema, no visual changes, no headaches, no cognitive changes or confusion, no falls, no fever, no night sweats, no significant unintentional weight loss. Appetite is fair.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vitals: Blood pressure 132/80 mmHg, heart rate 68 bpm, respiratory rate 14, temperature 36.8 degrees C, weight 73.2 kg (stable from last visit), BMI 26.4.

General: Well-appearing, somewhat thin older gentleman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Cooperative.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Mucous membranes moist. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical or supraclavicular regions.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, S1 and S2 normal. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing or crackles.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated. No palpable masses. Bowel sounds present.

Musculoskeletal/Extremities: No lower extremity pitting edema. Full range of motion in bilateral hips and knees. Mild paraspinal tenderness to deep palpation at the lower lumbar region (L3-L4 level), which the patient states is similar to his baseline and not worsening. No point tenderness over the right iliac crest today. No calf tenderness.

Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Gait steady and unassisted. No focal neurologic deficits.

Skin: No rash, no skin breakdown. Mild gynecomastia bilaterally (established side effect of ADT).

ECOG Performance Status: 1.

---

LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS

Today's labs:
- PSA: 0.8 ng/mL (nadir achieved; prior: 4.2 ng/mL at 3 months, 187.4 ng/mL at diagnosis)
- Testosterone: <10 ng/dL (confirmed castrate)
- CBC: WBC 6.1 K/uL, Hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, Platelets 218 K/uL
- CMP: Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 4.2 mEq/L, Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, eGFR 62 mL/min/1.73m2, ALT 22 U/L, AST 19 U/L, Bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, Alkaline phosphatase 98 U/L (mildly elevated, chronic)
- Blood pressure at visit: 132/80 mmHg (no significant hypertension)
- No hypokalemia, no significant fluid retention
- Lipid panel pending (due at this visit, ordered)

Note: Alkaline phosphatase has trended from 312 U/L at diagnosis (reflecting bone lesion activity) to 98 U/L today, consistent with ongoing bone healing response.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Repeat Tc-99m whole body bone scintigraphy (performed within the past 2 weeks): Known lesions at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala demonstrate sclerotic changes and decreased tracer uptake compared to baseline, consistent with treated and healing bone metastases. No new areas of abnormal radiotracer uptake identified. No superscan pattern. Impression: Stable to improved osseous disease in the setting of ongoing systemic therapy.

CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (concurrent with bone scan): No new visceral metastases identified. Liver, lungs, and adrenal glands are clear. Right external iliac lymph nodes have decreased in size from 1.8 cm to 0.9 cm. Prostate gland changes consistent with hormonal effect. No new lymphadenopathy.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 69-year-old gentleman with Stage IVB metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, Grade Group 5) with bony and lymph node metastases, now 12 months into treatment with ADT (leuprolide acetate) plus abiraterone/prednisone.

1. METASTATIC HORMONE-SENSITIVE PROSTATE CANCER - EXCELLENT RESPONSE:
PSA nadir has been achieved at 0.8 ng/mL, representing a >99% decline from his presenting PSA of 187.4 ng/mL. Testosterone confirmed castrate at <10 ng/dL. Repeat bone scan demonstrates no new lesions and sclerotic healing changes at known sites. CT imaging shows meaningful regression of pelvic lymphadenopathy (from 1.8 to 0.9 cm). This represents a deep and durable response to AR-pathway combination therapy. I have counseled the patient that while the current response is gratifying, mHSPC with Gleason 9 disease can progress to castration-resistant disease over time, and we will continue close surveillance. PSA at nadir is a favorable prognostic indicator.
- Continue leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months -- administered today.
- Continue abiraterone acetate 1000 mg orally daily with low-fat meal plus prednisone 5 mg BID. Patient to maintain consistent dosing schedule.
- Next PSA and testosterone check in 3 months.
- Surveillance imaging (bone scan and CT C/A/P) in 6 months or sooner if clinical concern arises.
- Note: PSA has begun a slight upward trend from nadir (1.1 ng/mL noted on lab around imaging; however today's value is 0.8 ng/mL and we will track trajectory carefully). Any two consecutive PSA rises above nadir should prompt evaluation for castration resistance.

2. FATIGUE (GRADE 1, ADT-RELATED):
Mild fatigue is expected and consistent with ongoing ADT. Contributing factors include anemia of chronic disease (Hgb 13.1, acceptable), hypogonadism from castration, and deconditioning.
- Encouraged continued light physical activity (daily walks, low-impact exercise).
- Reviewed sleep hygiene. Counseled regarding energy conservation strategies.
- No pharmacologic intervention warranted at this time given Grade 1 severity.
- Thyroid function to be checked at next visit if fatigue worsens.

3. HOT FLASHES (ADT-RELATED, CHRONIC):
Patient reports intermittent hot flashes, acceptable and manageable without intervention currently.
- Behavioral modifications discussed (cool environment, layered clothing, hydration).
- If worsening, could consider venlafaxine or gabapentin in the future.

4. BONE HEALTH:
Ongoing ADT creates risk for accelerated bone mineral density loss.
- Patient is already on calcium 1200 mg/day and vitamin D 1000 IU/day (confirmed at today's visit).
- DEXA scan due -- order placed today (baseline DEXA has not been documented in our records; requested prior records from referring urologist).
- No fractures reported clinically. Alkaline phosphatase trending down appropriately.

5. HYPERTENSION / CARDIOVASCULAR RISK:
Blood pressure 132/80 at today's visit. Managed by PCP with current regimen (lisinopril, per report). No hypokalemia on abiraterone.
- Continue current antihypertensive regimen. Potassium 4.2 mEq/L -- reassuring.
- Lipid panel ordered given ongoing cardiovascular risk in the setting of ADT (metabolic effects).
- Discussed lifestyle modifications.

6. GERMLINE GENETIC COUNSELING REFERRAL:
Given patient's family history (paternal prostate cancer at 78, paternal aunt with ovarian cancer) and his high-risk, high-grade metastatic disease presentation at age 68, germline testing for hereditary cancer syndromes (BRCA1/2, ATM, CHEK2, and related genes) is medically appropriate and clinically actionable. Results could have implications for treatment selection (eligibility for PARP inhibitors if pathogenic variant identified) and for family member cascade testing.
- Referral placed today for genetic counseling. Patient verbally consented to referral.
- Patient given written educational material regarding hereditary prostate cancer syndromes.

7. ADVANCE CARE PLANNING / QUALITY OF LIFE:
Patient is enjoying time with family, maintaining independence, and remains in good functional status. No acute goals-of-care discussions warranted at this time.
- Social work involvement not needed at present.
- Next visit scheduled in 3 months with PSA, testosterone, CMP, CBC.

All questions addressed. Patient verbalized understanding of the plan and will call with concerns. He will follow up with PCP in the interim for blood pressure management.

Time spent: approximately 40 minutes (examination, counseling, care coordination).

Electronically signed by: [Attending Genitourinary Medical Oncologist]
